items.title: Centor Criteria
items.contentHtml:
Centor Criteria

  • The Centor Criteria assign 1 point each for: fever >38°C, tonsillar exudate, painful anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and absence of cough.
  • A score of 0-1 suggests viral etiology (not antibiotics); 2-3 points indicates performing rapid strep test or culture; 4 points justify empirical treatment without testing.
  • In adults, a score ≥3 has a positive predictive value of ~40-60% for group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus infection.

items.title: Tonsillitis due to group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus
items.contentHtml:
Tonsillitis due to group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus

  • The causative agent is group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes), which produces toxins and enzymes such as streptolysin O and DNase B.
  • Clinically: abrupt onset, intense odynophagia, high fever, tonsillar exudate whitish, petechiae on the palate and painful cervical adenopathy.
  • Diagnostic confirmation is performed with rapid streptococcal test (specificity >95%) or culture of pharyngeal exudate (gold standard).

items.title: Peritonsillar abscess
items.contentHtml:
Peritonsillar abscess

  • Most common suppurative complication of bacterial tonsillitis, typically unilateral.
  • Presents: severe odynophagia, trismus (difficulty opening the mouth), “hot potato” voice and deviation of the uvula to the contralateral side.
  • The diagnosis is clinical; CT scan is reserved for doubtful cases. Treatment includes surgical drainage (puncture-aspiration or incision) plus antibiotics: intravenous penicillin or amoxicillin with clavulanic acid.

items.title: Differential diagnosis with infectious mononucleosis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with infectious mononucleosis

  • Infectious mononucleosis, caused by the Epstein-Barr virus, is differentiated by: prolonged fever, generalized lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly and intense fatigue.
  • Similar tonsillar exudate can be observed in the pharynx, but it is usually more membranous and accompanied by petechiae on the palate.
  • The presence of atypical lymphocytes in the peripheral blood smear and the positivity of the Monospot test (heterophilic antibodies) confirm the diagnosis.
  • Treatment with amoxicillin in mononucleosis can trigger a characteristic morbilliform rash.

items.title: Antibiotic treatment: penicillin or amoxicillin
items.contentHtml:
Antibiotic treatment: penicillin or amoxicillin

  • The first line for streptococcal tonsillitis is oral penicillin V 250 mg (children) or 500 mg (adults) every 8 hours for 10 days, or amoxicillin 50 mg/kg/day (max 1 g) every 12 hours for 10 days.
  • In patients allergic to penicillin, clindamycin 20 mg/kg/day every 8 hours for 10 days or a macrolide (azithromycin 5 days) is used.
  • Resistance to macrolides in group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus is increasing (10-20%), so they are preferred beta-lactams.

items.title: Indications for tonsillectomy
items.contentHtml:
Indications for tonsillectomy

  • Absolute indications include: recurrent peritonsillar abscess (≥2 episodes), obstructive tonsillar hypertrophy with sleep apnea or severe dysphagia, and suspected neoplasia.
  • Relative indications: ≥7 episodes of acute tonsillitis in the past year, or ≥5 per year for 2 consecutive years, or ≥3 per year for 3 years, documented and treated.
  • In children, tonsillectomy is considered when the frequency of infections impacts quality of life or causes recurrent school absences.

items.title: Rheumatic fever
items.contentHtml:
Rheumatic fever as a sequela

  • Rheumatic fever is a non-suppurative sequelae of group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus infection, mediated by molecular mimicry between the bacterial M protein and cardiac, joint and neurological tissue.
  • It occurs 2-4 weeks after untreated pharyngotonsillitis, with Jones criteria. modified: carditis, migratory polyarthritis, Sydenham's chorea, erythema marginatum and subcutaneous nodules.
  • Primary prevention is complete antibiotic treatment (10 days of penicillin or amoxicillin).

items.title: Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis
items.contentHtml:
Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis

  • Nonsuppurative sequelae of infection by nephritogenic strains of group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus (e.g. serotypes M12, M49), which occurs 1-3 weeks after pharyngitis or skin infection.
  • It is characterized by macroscopic hematuria, periorbital edema, arterial hypertension and oliguria, with decreased serum complement (low C3).
  • Treatment is supportive; Unlike rheumatic fever, poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis is not prevented by antibiotics, but management of the acute infection reduces the risk.

items.title: Scarlet fever
items.contentHtml:
Associated scarlet fever

  • Scarlet fever is a manifestation of group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus infection that produces an erythrogenic toxin (streptococcal pyrogenic exotoxin).
  • Clinically: fever, pharyngotonsillitis and bright red maculopapular (“sandpaper”) rash that spares the area perioral (perioral pallor) and peeling after 1-2 weeks.
  • The tongue may appear “strawberry” (prominent red papillae). Treatment is identical to that for strep throat: penicillin or amoxicillin for 10 days.

items.title: Recurrent tonsillitis in children
items.contentHtml:
Recurrent tonsillitis in children

  • It is defined as ≥7 episodes in a year, or ≥5 per year for 2 years, or ≥3 per year for 3 years, documented with symptoms and positive culture or rapid test.
  • Risk factors: age between 4-8 years, exposure to daycare, and asymptomatic carriers at home.
  • Management includes treatment complete antibiotic for each episode; Tonsillectomy is considered if frequency criteria are met and there is an impact on quality of life or complications.

items.title: rapid strep test
items.contentHtml:
Rapid Streptococcus Test

  • Detects group A carbohydrate antigen of group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus by enzyme immunoassay (EIA) or immunochromatography.
  • Sensitivity 85-95%, specificity >95%. A positive result confirms infection; A negative result in children or adolescents must be confirmed with pharyngeal culture (gold standard).
  • In adults with Centor Criteria <3, the rapid test is not indicated due to low pre-test probability.

items.title: Corticosteroids in acute tonsillitis
items.contentHtml:
Corticosteroids in acute tonsillitis

  • Corticosteroids (e.g. dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg, maximum 10 mg, single oral or intramuscular dose) reduce the duration of pain and fever in acute tonsillitis.
  • Its use is recommended as an adjuvant to antibiotics in cases of severe odynophagia or peritonsillar abscess, without increasing the risk of complications.
  • They are not routinely indicated in mild or viral tonsillitis. Evidence shows a reduction in pain at 24 hours in approximately 12 hours.
